tossups.question: While visiting a tomb after a long boat trip, this character ponders what to eat for lunch while imagining an old woman watching over the body of her grandson. The odes written by this character were first published in the short-lived Athena magazine and were compared to Horace’s in a fake review. In a novel, this character is described as constantly carrying and reading a copy of the fictional book The God of the Labyrinth. This character confesses his relationship struggles with Lydia and Marcendo during one of his periodic conversations with his creator. In that novel, this character dies by following his creator into the grave to the “other” Lisbon. For 10 points, what heteronym of Fernando Pessoa is the subject of a novel by José Saramago titled for the year of his death?
tossups.question_sanitized: While visiting a tomb after a long boat trip, this character ponders what to eat for lunch while imagining an old woman watching over the body of her grandson. The odes written by this character were first published in the short-lived Athena magazine and were compared to Horace's in a fake review. In a novel, this character is described as constantly carrying and reading a copy of the fictional book The God of the Labyrinth. This character confesses his relationship struggles with Lydia and Marcendo during one of his periodic conversations with his creator. In that novel, this character dies by following his creator into the grave to the "other" Lisbon. For 10 points, what heteronym of Fernando Pessoa is the subject of a novel by Jose Saramago titled for the year of his death?
tossups.answer_sanitized: Ricardo Reis [prompt on Fernando Pessoa until read]

packet.name: G - McMaster A, Notre Dame C, Rutgers A, NYU A

tossups.question: Over a glass of Colares Chita, this man cites Kafka to illustrate how “cowardice produced some of the bravest writing of the century.” A doctor stalks a man in a skeleton costume at Carnival after mistaking him for this man. A roaming author awaits a spirit of this man called the Guest in Antonio Tabucchi’s (“tah-BOO-keeʼs”) novel Requiem: A Hallucination. This man uses a womb-like period of nine months “to achieve total oblivion” in a novel that ends with the protagonist donning a jacket and following him into a cemetery. A man created by this author sleeps with the hotel maid Lydia and reads Herbert Quain’s The God of the Labyrinth aboard an ocean liner on his return from Brazil to a city where he converses with the ghost of this poet. For 10 points, José Saramago’s The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis depicts a “heteronym” of what Portuguese poet?
tossups.question_sanitized: Over a glass of Colares Chita, this man cites Kafka to illustrate how "cowardice produced some of the bravest writing of the century." A doctor stalks a man in a skeleton costume at Carnival after mistaking him for this man. A roaming author awaits a spirit of this man called the Guest in Antonio Tabucchi's ("tah-BOO-keeʼs") novel Requiem: A Hallucination. This man uses a womb-like period of nine months "to achieve total oblivion" in a novel that ends with the protagonist donning a jacket and following him into a cemetery. A man created by this author sleeps with the hotel maid Lydia and reads Herbert Quain's The God of the Labyrinth aboard an ocean liner on his return from Brazil to a city where he converses with the ghost of this poet. For 10 points, Jose Saramago's The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis depicts a "heteronym" of what Portuguese poet?
tossups.answer: Fernando Pessoa (“peh-SOH-uh”) [or Fernando António Nogueira de Seabra Pessoa; prompt on Ricardo Reis or Ricardo Reis until read; prompt on The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis or O Ano da Morte de Ricardo Reis until read]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Fernando Pessoa ("peh-SOH-uh") [or Fernando Antonio Nogueira de Seabra Pessoa; prompt on Ricardo Reis or Ricardo Reis until read; prompt on The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis or O Ano da Morte de Ricardo Reis until read]

tossups.question: The amnesiac protagonist, who forgets his life but remembers arcane literary facts, wakes up in Guanajuato with an ID card indicating that he is one of these figures in Jim Gauer’s Novel Explosives. Lesser known examples of these figures include the Baron of Tieve, whose insistence on living only according to reason led him to commit suicide, and the long-bearded astrologer Raphael Baldaya. One of these figures wrote “How should I know what I'll be, I who don't know what I am?” in the poem “The (*) Tobacco Shop.”One of these figures studied naval engineering in Scotland before becoming a Futurist and then a sentimentalist who wrote about his childhood. An Epicurean one of these figures exiled to Brazil following a failed 1919 rebellion talks to the ghost of the author who created him in a 1984 novel. Jose Saramago wrote that novel, titled for the “Year of the Death of” one of these figures. For 10 points, Alberto Caeiro, Bernardo Soares, and Ricardo Reis are among what set of names denoting differing styles used by the Portuguese author of The Book of Disquiet?
tossups.question_sanitized: The amnesiac protagonist, who forgets his life but remembers arcane literary facts, wakes up in Guanajuato with an ID card indicating that he is one of these figures in Jim Gauer's Novel Explosives. For 10 points, Alberto Caeiro, Bernardo Soares, and Ricardo Reis are among what set of names denoting differing styles used by the Portuguese author of The Book of Disquiet?

tossups.question: Scattered colored lines obscure a photo of this author on the cover of a 2020 New Directions edition of his “complete works” whose opening section, The Keeper of Sheep, was written on a “triumphant day.” This writer for the magazine Orpheus declared myth the “nothing that is everything” in the poem “Ulysses,” which is included in a book inspired by his country’s coat of arms. Richard Zenith and Margaret Jull Costa struggled to order a fragmentary book discovered posthumously in one of this author’s trunks that consists of a (*) bookkeeper’s musings framed as a “factless autobiography.” The ghost of this author encounters a doctor created by this author in the novel The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis, which was written by his compatriot José Saramago. For 10 points, name this Portuguese author who used one of his many “heteronyms” to write The Book of Disquiet.
tossups.answer: Fernando Pessoa [or Fernando António Nogueira Pessoa; accept Alberto Caeiro or Bernardo Soares; accept Ricardo Reis until read] (The book in the second sentence is Mensagem.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Fernando Pessoa [or Fernando Antonio Nogueira Pessoa; accept Alberto Caeiro or Bernardo Soares; accept Ricardo Reis until read] (The book in the second sentence is Mensagem.)
tossups.question_sanitized: Scattered colored lines obscure a photo of this author on the cover of a 2020 New Directions edition of his "complete works" whose opening section, The Keeper of Sheep, was written on a "triumphant day." The ghost of this author encounters a doctor created by this author in the novel The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis, which was written by his compatriot Jose Saramago. For 10 points, name this Portuguese author who used one of his many "heteronyms" to write The Book of Disquiet.

tossups.question: This man published a piece set in a “Forest of Estrangement” that later appeared in a larger work. This man encouraged “imagining an orgasm with Man A while copulating with Man B” in one text’s “Advice to Unhappily Married Women.” This man wrote that life’s great tragedies include having read The Pickwick Papers, because one can’t go back and read it a first time. This man wrote several fragments set during rainy days in the fourth-floor apartment of a man whose boss is named Vasques (“VASS-kess”). Many “Dolorous Interludes” dot an English translation of writing by this man which was compiled from over 500 sheets in a suitcase after his 1935 death. Richard Zenith translated a “factless autobiography” by this man credited to bookkeeper Bernardo Soares. For 10 points, name this man who ruminated on unease in The Book of Disquiet, a Portuguese author with many “heteronyms.”
tossups.answer: Fernando Pessoa [prompt on Vicente Guedes before “imagining”; prompt on Bernardo Soares; reject all other heteronyms as none but these two are relevant to the question]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Fernando Pessoa [prompt on Vicente Guedes before "imagining"; prompt on Bernardo Soares; reject all other heteronyms as none but these two are relevant to the question]

tossups.question: This man's translator Richard Zenith noted how, as a teenager in Durban, South Africa, he designed elaborate English-language newspapers filled with fake events. This author correctly prophesied his posthumous legacy in the line "roots may be hidden in the ground / But their flowers flower in the open air for all to see" in his poem "If I Die Young." A character created by this author dies in his mid-twenties from tuberculous after penning lines such as, "I thank God I'm not good." Margaret Jull Costa's "complete" edition of this poet's seminal collection is ordered chronologically, opening with a poem that declares, "My soul is a hidden orchestra." Many of the earliest poems in Jull's editions are purportedly by Vicente Guedes, whereas others are ascribed to a doctor named Ricardo Reis. For 10 points, name this Portuguese poet of The Book of Disquiet, known for his heteronyms.
tossups.answer: Fernando Pessoa [or Fernando António Nogueira Pessoa; accept Ricardo Reis or Vicente Guedes before mentioned; or Alberto Caeiro; or Bernardo Soares]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Fernando Pessoa [or Fernando Antonio Nogueira Pessoa; accept Ricardo Reis or Vicente Guedes before mentioned; or Alberto Caeiro; or Bernardo Soares]

tossups.question: In a short section of one work, this poet asserted that “to have touched the feet of Christ is no excuse for mistakes in punctuation”. This non-Tennyson poet described how “myth is the nothing that means everything” in his poem Ulisses. This best-known member of the Orpheu movement structured one (*) 44-poem collection around his nation’s coat of arms, and also wrote a lengthy “factless autobiography” about the isolated office clerk Bernardo Soares. A frequent user of “heteronyms” like Ricardo Reis, for 10 points, name this Portuguese poet of The Book of Disquiet and Message.

parts: Name this literary alter ego with a fictional biography as a polemic Glasgow-educated sailor and devotee of Walt Whitman. Poems credited to this author include “Ode of Triumph” and “The Tobacco Shop.”

Álvaro de Campos was one of the “heteronyms” used by Fernando Pessoa, a poet from this country. Pessoa’s heteronym Ricardo Reis was fictionalized in a novel by another author from this country, José Saramago.

parts: In Stefan Zweig’s (“ZVEIG's) novel The Royal Game, Dr. B develops the personalities White and Black while playing this game. At the novella’s climax, Dr. B has a mental breakdown and plays an illegal bishop check in this game.

parts_sanitized: In Stefan Zweig's ("ZVEIG's) novel The Royal Game, Dr. B develops the personalities White and Black while playing this game. At the novella's climax, Dr. B has a mental breakdown and plays an illegal bishop check in this game.
In this novel, Medardo of Terralba literally becomes two people, one good and one evil, after a cannonball splits him in half. This novel is the first in its author's trilogy Our Ancestors.
This Portuguese author used over 75 "heteronyms" like Alvaro do Campos and Ricardo Reis. This author used the heteronym Bernardo Soares to write the fragmentary work The Book of Disquiet.

bonuses.leadin: When asked if this character was real, his creator replies, “How real is life? How real is hate that poisons life?” in O Mostrengo, which is a section of verses dedicated to this character. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this spirit who tells the protagonist, “No matter how many vessels attempt the audacious passage you are plotting, my cape will be implacably hostile.”
Adamastor is the title character of O Mostrengo, which is part of this poet’s book Mensagem. This creator of several “heteronyms” wrote The Book of Disquiet.
Vasco da Gama encounters Adamastor in this country’s national epic, The Lusiads. Fernando Pessoa is from this Iberian country.
answers_sanitized: Adamastor
Fernando Pessoa [or Fernando Antonio Nogueira Pessoa]
Portugal [or the Portuguese Republic or Republica Portuguesa]

bonuses.leadin: A posthumously published book by this author follows a man who collects books he never reads and celebrates "sombre majesty of splendours no one knows." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author of a so-called "factless autobiography," which follows the bookkeeper Bernardo Soares. Soares is credited as the author of The Book of Disquiet, but is actually one of the several semi-heteronyms of this author.
Fernando Pessoa, the actual author of the Book of Disquiet, was born in this European country. This European country is the origin of The Lusiads, which follows Vasco de Gama's journey to India.

This Pessoa heteronym was a monarchist who went to exile in Brazil after a failed rebellion. He is the namesake of some odes by Pessoa, and Jose Saramago wrote a book about The Year of the Death of [this character].
answers: Ricardo Reis [accept either underlined name; or The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis]
answers_sanitized: Fernando Pessoa [or Fernando Antonio Nogueira Pessoa]
Portugal [or Portuguese Republic; or Republica Portuguesa]
Ricardo Reis [accept either underlined name; or The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis]

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, name these things about other Portuguese writers:
parts: This set of names includes Ricardo Reis, Alvaro de Campos, and Bernardo Soares, the latter of which nominally wrote The Book of Disquiet .

This playwright and probable goldsmith is alternately called the “Portuguese Plautus” and chronicles the relationships the “Father of Portuguese Drama.” His Trilogy of the Ships between those awaiting passage to Hell, Purgatory, or Heaven.
answers: Fernando Pessoa’s Heteronyms [synonyms for “Heteronyms” are fine, such as pseudonyms; pen-names; personalities; etc.]
answers_sanitized: Fernando Pessoa's Heteronyms [synonyms for "Heteronyms" are fine, such as pseudonyms; pen-names; personalities; etc.]
Gil Vicente

bonuses.leadin: A member of this group penned the lines “I am nothing / I shall never be anything / I cannot wish to be anything” in the poem “Tobacco Shop”, written after his time living in Newcastle. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this group that includes the Englishman Alexander Search, whose death at twenty was commemorated in an epigraph; the doctor-detective Abilio Quaresma; and the poet Alvaro de Campos.
One of Pessoa’s heteronyms outlives him in The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis, a novel by this Portuguese author, who wrote of an epidemic of “white sickness” in his novel Blindness.
Five people come together to travel across Iberia in this Saramago novel, in which the Iberian Peninsula breaks off from Europe and floats across the Atlantic.
answers: heteronyms of Fernando Pessoa [or alter ego, pseudonym, nom de plume, or other rough equivalents in place of “heteronym”]
answers_sanitized: heteronyms of Fernando Pessoa [or alter ego, pseudonym, nom de plume, or other rough equivalents in place of "heteronym"]
Jose Saramago [or Jose de Sousa Saramago]
The Stone Raft

bonuses.leadin: Ibis, Pip, and Nympha Negra are a few of this author’s many, many heteronyms. For 10 points each:
parts: This Portuguese author wrote of the “cold in Hadrian’s soul” in the poem “Antinous,” but is indubitably better known for his monumental Book of Disquiet.
Pessoa was perhaps influenced by this “Portuguese Plautus,” who penned the line “the pursuit of love / is like falconry” back in the 1500s.

This more recent Portuguese author wrote The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis, about another of Pessoa’s heteronyms. In another novel, he wrote of a woman who remains healthy as everyone else around her succumbs to blindness.
answers_sanitized: Fernando Pessoa
Gil Vicente
Jose Saramago
